title: Lamingtons
subtitle: Australian sponge squares in chocolate and coconut
excerpt: An Australian classic: squares of plain vanilla sponge dipped in chocolate icing and rolled in desiccated coconut. The dipping is easiest if the sponge is baked a day ahead and chilled. Makes sixteen.
sourceNotes: The lamington is documented as an Australian domestic baking preparation from the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, named after Lord Lamington, Governor of Queensland 1896–1901. The recipe appears in early Australian domestic cookbooks including the Commonsense Cookery Book (1915), produced by the Public School Cookery Teachers' Association of New South Wales, now in the public domain. The standard preparation of a plain vanilla butter cake, dipped in chocolate icing and coconut, is documented in multiple Australian home economics publications from the early twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze assembled lamingtons in a single layer, then bag, for up to 1 month.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the sponge a day ahead and refrigerate unwrapped overnight — the dry, firm surface dips better.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; the sponge is done when a skewer through the centre comes out clean and the top is golden

content.text: The lamington is about technique as much as recipe. A freshly baked sponge is too soft and crumbly to dip; one that has been refrigerated overnight is firm enough to handle. Work quickly through the dipping stage and have the coconut ready in a shallow tray so each piece can be rolled immediately after coating.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease and line a 20 × 20 cm square tin. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and creamy. Add the {{eggs}} one at a time with the {{vanilla-extract}}, beating after each. Fold in the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{whole-milk}} alternately. Pour into the tin and bake for 28–32 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tin, then refrigerate uncovered for at least 2 hours or overnight. Trim the crusts and cut into 16 equal squares.

Sift the {{icing-sugar}} and {{cocoa-powder}} into a bowl. Add the melted {{unsalted-butter}} and hot {{whole-milk}} and stir until smooth and fluid. The icing should be thin enough to coat easily; add a little more hot milk if it is too thick. Set up an assembly line: icing in a deep bowl, {{desiccated-coconut}} in a shallow tray, a rack over a tray to catch drips. Working two at a time, drop a sponge square into the icing, turn to coat all sides using two forks, then lift out and immediately roll in the coconut. Transfer to the rack and leave to set for 30 minutes.
